summary: proxy-check-i ships SSH backdoor daemon with hardcoded attacker key and reverse-tunnel C2
details: The package presents itself as a proxy-checking utility ('packaged command for running the bundled qsshd executable') but its console_scripts entry 'proxy-check-i' os.execv's a bundled Go SSH daemon (qsshd). The daemon embeds a single hardcoded ed25519 authorized_keys entry via go:embed and accepts only that key, granting whoever holds the matching private key full session/exec shell access plus arbitrary tcpip-forward and direct-tcpip port forwarding on the installer's host. The daemon builds a tunnel.Server (github.com/mydearniko/overthing) with auto-discovered RelayURI and interface-fallback dialer, establishing an outbound reverse tunnel so the backdoor is reachable through NAT/firewalls without any inbound listener. It also generates and persists an ed25519 device identity ('.device_lock') to ~/.config, /dev/shm, or /tmp (or accepts one via QSSHD_PRIVATE_KEY) so the relay operator can re-identify the host across reboots. The name/summary provide no indication of an SSH server, remote shell, reverse tunnel, or pinned authorized key — the cover story hides the payload. Installing and running proxy-check-i turns the host into a persistently controllable C2 node for the key holder.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
